title: Turkey national football team

infobox.value: |  | | --- | --- | |  | | | [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [2002 Korea and Japan](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") | [Team](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_squads#Turkey "2002 FIFA World Cup squads") | | [FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Confederations_Cup "FIFA Confederations Cup") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [2003 France](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup") | [Team](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup_squads#Turkey "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup squads") | | [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [2008 Austria and Switzerland](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008")[[b]](#cite_note-6) | [Team](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_squads#Turkey "UEFA Euro 2008 squads") | |

([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Millî Futbol Takımı*), recognized as **Türkiye** by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"),[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9) represents [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches. The team is controlled by the [Turkish Football Federation](/wiki/Turkish_Football_Federation "Turkish Football Federation") ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Futbol Federasyonu*), the governing body for [football in Turkey](/wiki/Football_in_Turkey "Football in Turkey"), which was founded in 1923 and has been a member of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") since 1923 and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") since 1962.[[8]](#cite_note-TFF_FIFA_UEFA_Status-10)

The **Türkiye national football team** ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Millî Futbol Takımı*), recognized as **Türkiye** by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"),[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9) represents [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches. The team is controlled by the [Turkish Football Federation](/wiki/Turkish_Football_Federation "Turkish Football Federation") ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Türkiye Futbol Federasyonu*), the governing body for [football in Turkey](/wiki/Football_in_Turkey "Football in Turkey"), which was founded in 1923 and has been a member of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") since 1923 and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") since 1962.[[8]](#cite_note-TFF_FIFA_UEFA_Status-10)

The team played their first official international game in 1923 and has represented the nation in major competitions since their debut appearance at the [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics"). They have participated in the Summer Olympics a total of six times ([1924](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics"), [1928](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics"), [1936](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"), [1948](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics"), [1952](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics"), and [1960](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics")), and reached the quarter-finals twice, in 1948 and 1952.

The team enjoyed their highest achievements in the 2000s, most notably finishing in third place at the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") and the [2003 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup"), and reaching the semi-finals at [UEFA Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"). They qualified for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") three times ([1950](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup"),[[c]](#cite_note-13) [1954](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup "1954 FIFA World Cup"), and [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup")) and reached the semi-finals in 2002, winning the bronze medal. The team qualified for the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") six times. Making their debut at [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"), they reached the quarter-finals in [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") and semi-finals in [Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"). In recent years, Turkey qualified to the [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), [Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"), and [Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024") championships, reaching the quarter-finals of the latter. Turkey was named as a co-host for [UEFA Euro 2032](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2032 "UEFA Euro 2032"), and will qualify automatically.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Ilkmac1923.jpg/250px-Ilkmac1923.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilkmac1923.jpg)

Turkey against [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") in 1923

The Turkish national team's first game was against [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team"), played on 26 October 1923 at [Taksim Stadium](/wiki/Taksim_Stadium "Taksim Stadium") in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), a 2–2 draw.[[11]](#cite_note-14) [Zeki Rıza Sporel](/wiki/Zeki_R%C4%B1za_Sporel "Zeki Rıza Sporel") is considered as the first big star of Turkish football as he scored the first two goals against Romania. Turkey played their first ever official match at the [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1924 Summer Olympics") losing to [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team"), 5–2. The first two goals in an official game were scored by [Bekir Refet](/wiki/Bekir_Refet "Bekir Refet").

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Turkey_national_football_team_%2828.05.1950%29.png/250px-Turkey_national_football_team_%2828.05.1950%29.png)](/wiki/File:Turkey_national_football_team_(28.05.1950).png)

Turkey in 1950

Although Turkey qualified for the [1950 World Cup](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup"), beating [Syria](/wiki/Syria_national_football_team "Syria national football team") 7–0, they were forced to withdraw from the tournament due to financial problems.

Turkey then qualified for the [1954 World Cup](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup "1954 FIFA World Cup") after a play-off with [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"). They first lost 4–1 to Spain, but a 1–0 win a few days later initiated a replay (there was no clause for aggregate results in the rules at the time, which would have qualified Spain). On that occasion, they tied 2–2 after, booking their place after a coin toss. Turkey was grouped along with [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") and [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"). The Turks, however, never played Hungary due to the tournament format, and a 4–1 defeat by the Germans was followed by Turkey carrying out a 7–0 win over [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team"). Turkey lost the play-off game to West Germany 7–2.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Alemania_v_Turqu%C3%ADa%2C_Estadio%2C_1954-08-07_%28586%29.jpg/250px-Alemania_v_Turqu%C3%ADa%2C_Estadio%2C_1954-08-07_%28586%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alemania_v_Turqu%C3%ADa,_Estadio,_1954-08-07_(586).jpg)

Turkey against West Germany at the 1954 World Cup

In 1956, however, Turkey did play Hungary in a friendly match in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), defeating [what was one of the strongest teams of the era](/wiki/Golden_Team "Golden Team"), 3–1.[[12]](#cite_note-15) [Lefter Küçükandonyadis](/wiki/Lefter_K%C3%BC%C3%A7%C3%BCkandonyadis "Lefter Küçükandonyadis"), arguably one of the best Turkish strikers of all time, scored two goals during the tournament.

### Near misses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Near misses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Voetbal_Nederland_tegen_Turkije_in_het_Olympisch_stadion_Elftallen%2C_Bestanddeelnr_909-5589.jpg/250px-Voetbal_Nederland_tegen_Turkije_in_het_Olympisch_stadion_Elftallen%2C_Bestanddeelnr_909-5589.jpg)](/wiki/File:Voetbal_Nederland_tegen_Turkije_in_het_Olympisch_stadion_Elftallen,_Bestanddeelnr_909-5589.jpg)

Turkey against [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") in 1958

Despite the introduction of [a national league](/wiki/S%C3%BCper_Lig "Süper Lig"), and showings by Turkish clubs in European competition, the 1960s would be a barren time for the national team. Most players from the 1954 World Cup squad had already retired, and the new generation of players failed to qualify for a major tournament.

The 1970s saw Turkey holding back in the World Cup and [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") qualifiers, but the team was a point too short to qualify for both [UEFA Euro 1972](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1972 "UEFA Euro 1972") and [Euro 1976](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1976 "UEFA Euro 1976").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/17/Co%C5%9Fkun_%C3%96zar%C4%B1_Coach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Co%C5%9Fkun_%C3%96zar%C4%B1_Coach.jpg)

[Coşkun Özarı](/wiki/Co%C5%9Fkun_%C3%96zar%C4%B1 "Coşkun Özarı") coached Turkey in the 1970s and 1980s

In the 1980s the Turks also suffered one of their worst defeats with 8–0 scorelines twice against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"). The Turks had suffered an 8–0 defeat before against [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") in 1968. But the [1990 World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") qualifiers would mark a turning point for Turkish football, with Turkey only missing out on qualification in the final match. Prominent players in this period included [Rıdvan Dilmen](/wiki/R%C4%B1dvan_Dilmen "Rıdvan Dilmen"), [Oğuz Çetin](/wiki/O%C4%9Fuz_%C3%87etin "Oğuz Çetin"), [Rıza Çalımbay](/wiki/R%C4%B1za_%C3%87al%C4%B1mbay "Rıza Çalımbay"), [Feyyaz Uçar](/wiki/Feyyaz_U%C3%A7ar "Feyyaz Uçar"), and [European Golden Boot](/wiki/European_Golden_Shoe "European Golden Shoe") winner [Tanju Çolak](/wiki/Tanju_%C3%87olak "Tanju Çolak").

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In 1990, coach [Sepp Piontek](/wiki/Sepp_Piontek "Sepp Piontek") was put in charge of the national team. Under his guidance, a group of new players debuted for the national team. Many of these players (which included [Bülent Korkmaz](/wiki/B%C3%BClent_Korkmaz "Bülent Korkmaz"), [Alpay Özalan](/wiki/Alpay_%C3%96zalan "Alpay Özalan"), [Sergen Yalçın](/wiki/Sergen_Yal%C3%A7%C4%B1n "Sergen Yalçın"), [Rüştü Reçber](/wiki/R%C3%BC%C5%9Ft%C3%BC_Re%C3%A7ber "Rüştü Reçber"), and [Hakan Şükür](/wiki/Hakan_%C5%9E%C3%BCk%C3%BCr "Hakan Şükür")) would become the backbone of the national team for many years. Piontek's mission came to an end in 1993, when he was replaced by [Fatih Terim](/wiki/Fatih_Terim "Fatih Terim"), who in turn managed to qualify for [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"). Turkey qualified for its first major tournament since 1954. The appointment of Piontek was a recommended move by another coach, [Jupp Derwall](/wiki/Jupp_Derwall "Jupp Derwall"), who had coached [Galatasaray](/wiki/Galatasaray_S.K._(football) "Galatasaray S.K. (football)") for three seasons.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1996-425.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_1996-425.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Azerbaijan,_1996-425.jpg)

Turkish national football team on an [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") stamp for [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996")

Turkey qualified for Euro 1996, defeating both [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") 2–1 during qualification. Despite a solid performance during the [qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996_qualifying "UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying"), Turkey lost all their matches without scoring a single goal. They did, however, go home with an award: the fair play award, given to Özalan.

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Although Turkey failed to qualify for the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), they qualified for [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") after winning a play-off against the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team"). Turkey lost their first match 2–1 to [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), then drew their second match against [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") 0–0, and beat host nation [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") 2–0, making it the first time in the history of the UEFA European Championship a host nation had been eliminated in the first round. This victory brought Turkey into the quarter-finals of the tournament, where they were beaten 2–0 by [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"), with [Arif Erdem](/wiki/Arif_Erdem "Arif Erdem") missing a penalty.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Belgium-Turkey_2000-06-19.svg/250px-Belgium-Turkey_2000-06-19.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Belgium-Turkey_2000-06-19.svg)

Line-ups for the UEFA Euro 2000 game between Belgium and Turkey at the [King Baudouin Stadium](/wiki/King_Baudouin_Stadium "King Baudouin Stadium"), Brussels on 19 June 2000

For the [2002 World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), Turkey finished second in their qualifying group, losing to Sweden in the match that would decide the number-one spot. The Turks were forced to play the play-offs against [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team"). They defeated the Austrians 6–0 on aggregate and booked their place in the finals. The Turkish team started the 2002 World Cup with a 2–1 defeat against [eventual winners](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_Final "2002 FIFA World Cup Final") [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team").[[13]](#cite_note-16) Turkey qualified from the group stage with a 3–0 win against [China PR](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") after drawing 1–1 with [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica_national_football_team "Costa Rica national football team").[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18) Turkey then faced co-hosts [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team") in the second round, winning 1–0.[[16]](#cite_note-19) The Turkish team continued their run, as they beat [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal_national_football_team "Senegal national football team") 1–0 on a [golden goal](/wiki/Golden_goal "Golden goal") to book their place in the semi-finals, where a 1–0 defeat against eventual tournament winners Brazil forced them to play the third-place match. The Turks won the bronze medal after a 3–2 victory over co-hosts South Korea.[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22) Hakan Şükür scored Turkey's first goal in 10.8 seconds, despite the South Koreans kicking off first. It was the fastest goal in World Cup history.[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)
Tens of thousands of flag-waving Turkish fans greeted the World Cup squad on their return to [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), where they joined a massive street party at [Taksim Square](/wiki/Taksim_Square "Taksim Square").[[22]](#cite_note-25) Rüştü Reçber, Alpay Özalan and [Hasan Şaş](/wiki/Hasan_%C5%9Ea%C5%9F "Hasan Şaş") were all included in the [All-Star Team](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_awards "FIFA World Cup awards"), with Reçber also being voted as the best goalkeeper in the [UEFA Team of the Year 2002](/wiki/UEFA_Team_of_the_Year "UEFA Team of the Year"), while [Şenol Güneş](/wiki/%C5%9Eenol_G%C3%BCne%C5%9F "Şenol Güneş") was being voted as the best manager.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_2002-627.jpg/330px-Stamps_of_Azerbaijan%2C_2002-627.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Azerbaijan,_2002-627.jpg)

The Turkish national team on an [Azerbaijani](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") stamp for the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup")

In 2003, Turkey finished third at the [FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup"). In the group stage, Turkey defeated the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") 2–1 before losing to [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") 1–0. In their final group match, Turkey drew 2–2 against Brazil, eliminating them from the tournament. Turkey lost to eventual tournament winners [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") 3–2 in the semi-finals, and then defeated [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia_national_football_team "Colombia national football team") 2–1 in the third-place match. [Tuncay](/wiki/Tuncay_(footballer) "Tuncay (footballer)") scored three goals and an assist, which won him the Silver Shoe Award and the Silver Ball Award for the second-best player of the tournament.

The Turks failed to qualify for [Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004") after [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team") won the qualification play-offs. They also missed out on the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup") after failing to win the play-offs, this time on away goals against Switzerland, again after finishing second in their group.

Turkey qualified for their first international tournament in six years by finishing second behind [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") in [Euro 2008 qualifying Group C](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying_Group_C "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group C") to reach [the tournament](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"). They were placed alongside Switzerland, Portugal, and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") in [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2008 Group A"). In their first match, they played Portugal and were beaten 2–0, but wins over Switzerland (2–1) and the Czech Republic (3–2) – both secured by late goals – meant qualification for the knockout stages.[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)[[25]](#cite_note-28) Turkey knocked out a host nation – Switzerland – in the group stages for the second time.[[26]](#cite_note-29)

The quarter-final against [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team") was goalless after 90 minutes, and Croatia led 1–0 in the final minute of extra time, but another late Turkish goal by forward [Semih Şentürk](/wiki/Semih_%C5%9Eent%C3%BCrk "Semih Şentürk") brought the game to penalties. The goal raised some controversy with Croatia fans and Croatia head coach [Slaven Bilić](/wiki/Slaven_Bili%C4%87 "Slaven Bilić"), who claimed that the goal had been scored after extra time had elapsed. This complaint, however, was overruled, and the game went into penalties. Turkey defeated Croatia in penalties, 3–1.[[27]](#cite_note-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/GER-TUR_2008-06-25.svg/250px-GER-TUR_2008-06-25.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GER-TUR_2008-06-25.svg)

Line-ups for the UEFA Euro 2008 match between Germany and Turkey on 25 June 2008 at [St. Jakob-Park](/wiki/St._Jakob-Park "St. Jakob-Park"), Basel

Turkey went into the semi-final against Germany with just 14 outfield players available as a result of injuries and suspensions but scored first and were drawing 2–2. But they finished third by default after losing 3–2 with a last-minute goal by [Philipp Lahm](/wiki/Philipp_Lahm "Philipp Lahm").[[28]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/5_juin_2009%2C_stade_Gerland_%C3%A0_Lyon._France_-_Turquie_%281-0%29.jpg/250px-5_juin_2009%2C_stade_Gerland_%C3%A0_Lyon._France_-_Turquie_%281-0%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:5_juin_2009,_stade_Gerland_%C3%A0_Lyon._France_-_Turquie_(1-0).jpg)

Turkey against [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") on 5 June 2009

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010s")]

Turkey was drawn in [UEFA Group 5](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_5 "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 5") together with [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_national_football_team "Armenia national football team"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team"), [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia_national_football_team "Estonia national football team") and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"). Turkey had a mixed qualifying campaign, finishing with 15 points and missing out on a play-off place to Bosnia and Herzegovina with 19 points. Spain topped the group to qualify, winning every game in the process. Coach Fatih Terim announced he would be resigning his post following their failure to qualify.[[29]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Tuerkische_Fu%C3%9Fballnationalmannschaft_2011-09-06_%2802%29.jpg/250px-Tuerkische_Fu%C3%9Fballnationalmannschaft_2011-09-06_%2802%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tuerkische_Fu%C3%9Fballnationalmannschaft_2011-09-06_(02).jpg)

The Turks during Euro 2012 qualification

Turkey was drawn in [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying Group A") in [qualification](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying") for [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), together with [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_national_football_team "Kazakhstan national football team"), Austria, Belgium, Germany and [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_national_football_team "Azerbaijan national football team"). The Turks reached the play-offs after beating Azerbaijan 1–0 but were eliminated 3–0 on aggregate by Croatia.

On 14 November 2012, Turkey celebrated their 500th match in a friendly game played against [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team") at the [Türk Telekom Arena](/wiki/T%C3%BCrk_Telekom_Arena "Türk Telekom Arena"), Istanbul, which ended in a 1–1 draw. Before the match, footballers and coaches, who contributed to the national team's success in the past, were honored. Turkish pop singer [Hadise](/wiki/Hadise "Hadise"), who wore a national team jersey with the number 500, performed a small concert.[[30]](#cite_note-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34)

Turkey was drawn in [Group D](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_D "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group D") in [qualification](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualifying "2014 FIFA World Cup qualifying") for the [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), together with [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra_national_football_team "Andorra national football team"), Estonia, Hungary, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") and Romania, finishing fourth. [Abdullah Avcı](/wiki/Abdullah_Avc%C4%B1 "Abdullah Avcı") was sacked soon after. Fatih Terim was put in charge for the third time to lead the national team, but a 2–0 defeat against the Netherlands ended hopes of qualification.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/AUT_vs._TUR_2016-03-29_%28001%29.jpg/250px-AUT_vs._TUR_2016-03-29_%28001%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUT_vs._TUR_2016-03-29_(001).jpg)

Turkey against [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") on 29 March 2016

Grouped in [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group A") in the [qualification campaign](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying") for [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), with [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team"), Latvia, Kazakhstan, the Netherlands, and the Czech Republic, the team qualified for their first major tournament in eight years as the best third-placed team after beating Iceland 1–0, with [Selçuk İnan](/wiki/Sel%C3%A7uk_%C4%B0nan "Selçuk İnan") netting a free kick in the 89th minute, along with Kazakhstan beating Latvia 1–0.[[32]](#cite_note-35)[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39) After a year and a half of going unbeaten, a loss to England as a pre-tournament friendly ended the team's winning streak, subsequently leading to back-to-back losses against Croatia and Spain in the tournament. Turkey won their last game against the Czech Republic, 2–0. They nearly reached the last 16 until a late winner for Ireland against Italy meant that the latter instead qualified as one of the best third-placed teams.[[37]](#cite_note-40)

Placed with Croatia, [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team"), Iceland, [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_national_football_team "Kosovo national football team") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") for [2018 World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), manager Fatih Terim left after an off-field incident,[[38]](#cite_note-41) and 72-year-old former Romania manager [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu") took over. After seven matches, Turkey were in pole position to qualify for the tournament, First, they boosted their World Cup hopes by beating Croatia 1–0, but a 3–0 defeat to Iceland at home ended automatic qualification hopes. After a 2–2 draw against Finland, the team finished fourth in Group I.

Turkey was drawn with [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") and Sweden in the [2018–19 UEFA Nations League B](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2018–19 UEFA Nations League B") and performed poorly, leading to a bottom finish. In the first game at home against 2018 World Cup host and quarter-finalist Russia, Turkey lost 2–1. Turkey then put up its best performance in the League, winning 3–2 against Sweden on Swedish soil. However, Turkey could not extend their performances and lost to Russia in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi") before suffering a humiliating 1–0 home loss to Sweden, thus initially relegating Turkey to League C. However, UEFA rule changes meant Turkey was allowed to remain in League B.

Turkey was drawn in Group H in the qualifying stages of Euro 2020 along with 2018 World Cup champions France, Iceland, [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team"), [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova_national_football_team "Moldova national football team"), and Andorra. Veteran coach [Şenol Güneş](/wiki/%C5%9Eenol_G%C3%BCne%C5%9F "Şenol Güneş") revolutionized the team, with many young talents, combining them with experienced players like [Burak Yılmaz](/wiki/Burak_Y%C4%B1lmaz "Burak Yılmaz") and [Emre Belözoglu](/wiki/Emre_Bel%C3%B6zoglu "Emre Belözoglu"). The team eventually managed to achieve a 2–0 victory against France in [Konya](/wiki/Konya "Konya") and a 1–1 draw at the Stade de France. Turkey then struggled against Andorra in their first match, winning after an 89th-minute goal at [Vodafone Park](/wiki/Vodafone_Park "Vodafone Park") in Istanbul. Turkey's only defeat in the group came against Iceland in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), losing 2–1. The defeat came after the ill-treatment of the Turkish group at the Iceland customs, keeping them at the airport for three hours. This was followed by an Icelandic supporter holding a toilet brush to team captain Emre Belozoglu as a pretend microphone during an interview. The events were heavily criticized by the Turkish and European media. Turkey entered matchday nine as group leaders with 19 points. They and Iceland drew 0–0 at [Turk Telekom Arena](/wiki/Turk_Telekom_Arena "Turk Telekom Arena") in Istanbul. After finishing behind France, a draw was enough to secure Turkey a spot in [Euro 2020 finals](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"), ahead of their away match against Andorra.[[39]](#cite_note-42)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Baku_Olympic_Stadium_hosted_final_stage_of_European_Football_Championship_Turkey-Wales_Azerbaijani_and_Turkish_presidents_watched_the_game%2C_June_2021_10.jpg/250px-Baku_Olympic_Stadium_hosted_final_stage_of_European_Football_Championship_Turkey-Wales_Azerbaijani_and_Turkish_presidents_watched_the_game%2C_June_2021_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baku_Olympic_Stadium_hosted_final_stage_of_European_Football_Championship_Turkey-Wales_Azerbaijani_and_Turkish_presidents_watched_the_game,_June_2021_10.jpg)

Turkey during the [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020") group stage match against [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") in [Baku](/wiki/Baku "Baku")

After qualifying for Euro 2020, which would be postponed to June and July 2021 due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"), Turkey was drawn against Russia, alongside [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team"), and [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team") in the [2020–21 UEFA Nations League B](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2020–21 UEFA Nations League B"). They performed poorly in their two opening games in September 2020. The first game against Hungary at home saw Turkey suffer a 1–0 defeat courtesy of a free kick from [Dominik Szoboszlai](/wiki/Dominik_Szoboszlai "Dominik Szoboszlai"). Playing in [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"), following [Aleksandar Kolarov](/wiki/Aleksandar_Kolarov "Aleksandar Kolarov")'s red card, the Turks were held goalless against Serbia. This hampered their chances of qualifying for League A, as their next opponents in October would be Russia (a country that Turkey hasn't beaten since 1975) and Hungary.

In [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), Turkey drew 1–1 after [Kenan Karaman](/wiki/Kenan_Karaman "Kenan Karaman")'s equalizer. The Turkish side then drew 2–2 against Serbia at home.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

Against Russia, [Denis Cheryshev](/wiki/Denis_Cheryshev "Denis Cheryshev") scored for the Russians to take an early lead making the Turkish side look hapless. But after Russia's red card reduced the side to ten men, the Turks eventually won 3–2.[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45)[[43]](#cite_note-46) The Turks then traveled to Hungary with the hope that a win against the Hungarians could mean possible promotion, if Russia lost to Serbia. While Russia suffered a humiliating 5–0 defeat away in Belgrade, Turkey also lost 2–0 to the Hungarians. That meant Turkey and Serbia were tied on points, but they were relegated after they lost the head-to-head away goals tiebreaker, 2–0.[[44]](#cite_note-47)[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49) Such an outcome also meant Turkey would have to fight to get a direct [Qatar 2022](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") ticket as play-off qualification appeared slim with their relegation, in which the [2022 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") that started in March 2021.

Turkey had the honor of opening the Euro 2021 finals, but with [Ciro Immobile](/wiki/Ciro_Immobile "Ciro Immobile") and [Lorenzo Insigne](/wiki/Lorenzo_Insigne "Lorenzo Insigne") scoring, Turkey fell 3–0 to Italy. [Roberto Mancini](/wiki/Roberto_Mancini "Roberto Mancini")'s side had been frustrated by Turkey's defensive approach in the first half but broke through in the 53rd minute when a hard-hit cross from [Domenico Berardi](/wiki/Domenico_Berardi "Domenico Berardi") flew in off Turkish defender [Merih Demiral](/wiki/Merih_Demiral "Merih Demiral") for an own goal. They went on to lose the next two games to both [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team"), losing all three games alongside debutants [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team").

In the [2022 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), Turkey was drawn into [Group G](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_G "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group G") with [the Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro_national_football_team "Montenegro national football team"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team"), and [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar_national_football_team "Gibraltar national football team"). After they finished second in their group behind the Netherlands,[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52) they entered [2022 FIFA World Cup UEFA playoffs](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round") against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team")[[50]](#cite_note-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54) but they lost 3–1 in a game that [Burak Yılmaz](/wiki/Burak_Y%C4%B1lmaz "Burak Yılmaz") missed a crucial penalty to equalise and thus failed to qualify for the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup").[[52]](#cite_note-55) Afterwards, he announced his international retirement.[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57)

In [2022–23 UEFA Nations League C](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Nations_League_C "2022–23 UEFA Nations League C"), they were drawn in Group 1 with [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team"), [the Faroe Islanders](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team"). They were promoted to [League B](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League "2024–25 UEFA Nations League") after recording 13 points in six games. Their Nations League success not only rewarded them a playoff place should they fail to qualify directly,[[55]](#cite_note-58) but also an easier draw in Group D with Croatia, Wales, [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_national_football_team "Armenia national football team"), and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team"). They subsequently qualified directly on 16 October 2023 and finished first on 19 November 2023.[[56]](#cite_note-59)[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61)

Turkey took part in [Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024") Group F together with [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") and play-off winner [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team") and finished the group in 2nd place. The team reached the quarter-finals by beating [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") 2–1, then lost to the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") 2–1 and finished the tournament in 5th place.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

In the [2026 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), Turkey was drawn into [Group E](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_E "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group E") alongside Euro 2024 champions [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team"), and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team").[[60]](#cite_note-63) Although Turkey managed a 2–2 away draw against Spain in their final group match in November 2025 and finished second in the group, they were unable to overcome Spain’s superior goal difference, largely as a result of a 0–6 home defeat earlier in September. Consequently, Turkey qualified for the play-offs for the [second consecutive time](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round").[[61]](#cite_note-64) They were drawn into [Path C](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round#Path_C "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round") and are scheduled to face [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") in a single-leg semi-final home match in Istanbul on 26 March 2026. If Turkey advance, they will play away against the winner of the [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team") vs. [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") match on 31 March 2026. The winner of the final will qualify for the World Cup and join [Group D](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_Group_D "2026 FIFA World Cup Group D") alongside co-host [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team"), and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team"). Should Turkey qualify, it would mark their first World Cup appearance since 2002.[[62]](#cite_note-65)

### 2030s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2030s")]

Turkey is set to take part of the [UEFA Euro 2032](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2032 "UEFA Euro 2032") having automatically qualified as co-host along with Italy who also automatically qualified as co-host.

Turkey's kit colors were home red kits and white away kits, manufactured by [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), from 1983 to 2002. [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") became the manufacturer in 2003. 17 years later, they switched their kit colors to white for home kits and red as the away color.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Turkey has developed several notable rivalries, the most well-known being [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team")[[63]](#cite_note-66) and [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team").

Turkey and Croatia have played each other nine times,[[64]](#cite_note-67) with their first encounter at [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"); where both countries made their debuts in the opening match, which Croatia won 1–0.[[65]](#cite_note-68) In [Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"), Turkey won on penalties after a 1–1 deadlock. The two teams faced each other in the [2012 Euro qualifying play-offs](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying"), with Croatia winning 3–0 in the first-leg in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), and advancing to the tournament finals following a 0–0 draw in the second-leg.[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70) The two teams faced each other once again in a European competition at [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), playing in the opening match of [Group D](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016#Group_D "UEFA Euro 2016"), with Croatia winning 1–0.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) Only three months after the Euros, the two teams played in their opening match in [Group I of 2018 FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_I "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group I"), which finished 1–1.[[70]](#cite_note-73) Exactly one year later, Turkey won the reverse fixture 1–0 at home, which played a key part in both countries' qualifying campaigns, although Turkey would not qualify for the World Cup while Croatia would finish runners-up behind France.[[71]](#cite_note-74)[[72]](#cite_note-75) Turkey and Croatia would again face each other during the [UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying") campaign, where Turkey managed to shock Croatia with a 1–0 win away as the Turks managed to top the table of a qualification group for the first time in their history, whereas Croatia, despite a 2–0 away win earlier to Turkey, had struggled with a rather poor performance and only managed to qualify at the last hurdle.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)

Turkey also has a historical rivalry with Greece. Playing 13 times, Turkey won eight matches, drew thrice, and lost only three games.[[75]](#cite_note-78) Both countries have been described as "punching above their weight", with Greece winning [Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004") after being classified as underdogs before the competition, and Turkey advancing to the semi-finals of [Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"), where they were knocked out by [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"). Due to tension between the two countries and the dispute over [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), coupled with several incidents occurring during matches between Turkish and Greek clubs, it has been described as one of the biggest international football rivalries.[[76]](#cite_note-79)

### Decoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turkey_national_football_team&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Decoration")]

In 2002, the national team was honored with the Turkish "[State Medal of Distinguished Service](/wiki/State_Medal_of_Distinguished_Service "State Medal of Distinguished Service")" for their third place achievement at the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"). All the team members, coaches and officials were given medals.[[94]](#cite_note-103)
